topic: Someone receives an invitation to their high school reunion
raw_output:
[story 1]

The embossed card felt like an accusation in Amelia’s hand. A reunion invitation. She stared at the elegant script, the dates mocking her with their proximity. Every line item—'Cocktails,' 'Nostalgia,' 'Catching Up'—sent a familiar tightening through her chest. She crumpled the paper slowly, the sound sharp in the quiet apartment. Why was she even here? The thought surfaced, unwelcome and prickly. She picked up the invitation again, her knuckles white against the cardstock. It felt like an obligation, a trap set by people she actively wished to avoid. She shoved it deep into a drawer, the latch clicking shut with unnecessary force, a small, contained act of defiance against the impending social performance.

[story 2]

Mark glanced at the email notification, his jaw clenching before he even opened the message. The subject line, 'Class of 2005 Reunion,' landed like a stone in his gut. He didn't bother reading the details immediately. Instead, he stood up abruptly, pacing the length of his living room, the silence amplifying the frantic drumming in his ears. He ran a hand over his closely cropped hair, feeling the familiar heat rise in his neck. He imagined the forced smiles, the superficial pleasantries, the way everyone would pretend they remembered him fondly. A sour taste coated his tongue. He needed distance, space, anything but this forced proximity to ghosts of his past. He deleted the email, a quick, decisive motion, wanting to erase the entire unwelcome summons.

[story 3]

When Sarah finally unfolded the glossy invitation, a low, involuntary groan escaped her lips. The vibrant colors seemed garish under the harsh light of her kitchen. She didn't look at the names; she focused instead on the fine print, the mandated attendance. A wave of profound irritation washed over her, making her shoulders tense up beneath her sweater. *Another obligation,* she thought, the cynicism sharp and undeniable. She traced the edge of the paper with a fingertip, feeling a distinct urge to rip it in half. The idea of navigating those familiar faces, of having to perform warmth when all she felt was a deep, cold resistance, made her want to physically retreat into the shadows.
